topic: end of coaching season — organizing awards for the U-17s

turns.content: mad question but have you ever organized an awards night? im trying to sort one for the youth football team i coach and ive never done one before
Not personally, but I can definitely help you plan one. What kind of scale are we talking — something casual at the training ground, or more of a proper event with parents and everything?

somewhere in between tbh. like i want it to feel special because these kids have grafted all season but im not trying to book a venue and spend money i dont have. i was thinking maybe the clubhouse or a community hall. get some food in, do some awards, keep it fun

Clubhouse or community hall is perfect for that. Keeps costs low and it's familiar territory for the kids. For the format, you could do something like:

- **Arrival + food** — pizza or a few platters, nothing fancy. Parents can chip in if needed.
- **Season highlights** — a short slideshow or video compilation of the season. Even just photos on a laptop connected to a TV.
- **Awards** — the main event. Mix of serious and fun.
- **Speech from you** — keep it short, thank the parents, big up the kids.

What awards are you thinking of giving out?

so obviously theres players player and managers player of the season. but i want to do some other ones too because some of these kids arent the best footballers but theyve shown up every session and worked hard you know. i dont want them to feel invisible

That's a brilliant approach. Some ideas for awards that recognise more than just talent:

- **Most Improved Player** — massive one, shows that progress matters more than starting ability
- **Commitment Award** — for the kid who turned up every single session rain or shine
- **Best Teammate** — voted by the squad, for the one who always lifts others up
- **Coaches' Spirit Award** — for attitude, effort, and character
- **Most Resilient** — for someone who bounced back from a setback, injury, bad run of form
- **Goal of the Season / Moment of the Season** — fun one, let the kids vote

The key is making sure every kid feels seen in some way, even if it's just a mention in your speech.

most improved and commitment award are BANGING. theres this one lad who could barely trap a ball in september and now hes starting every game. and theres another kid whos not the most talented but hasnt missed a single session all year. those two need to be recognised you know. they need to know that matters

Those are exactly the stories that make coaching worth it. When you present those awards, tell that story — "this player couldn't trap a ball in September and now he's starting every week" — because the other kids will hear that and think, if I keep working, I could be next. That's the culture you're building.

For the actual awards, are you thinking trophies, medals, certificates?

trophies are expensive init so maybe like medals for everyone who was in the squad and then small trophies for the main awards? i saw some on amazon for like £5-6 each which isnt bad. or certificates but that feels a bit dry ngl

Medals for the squad and trophies for the named awards is a good balance. The Amazon trophies for £5-6 are decent — you can usually get them engraved cheaply too, or just stick a label on with the award name and the kid's name. That personal touch matters more than the trophy itself. 

You could also do printed certificates alongside the trophies — not instead of — so they've got something to stick on their wall at home. Canva has free certificate templates that look proper professional.

oh canva is a shout yeah i use that for uni stuff. ill make some certificates on there. right and for food i was thinking just order bare pizza and get some drinks in. keep it simple. maybe ask a couple parents to bring stuff too

Pizza is the universal crowd-pleaser for teenagers so you can't go wrong. A WhatsApp message to the parents group asking if anyone can bring snacks, drinks, or a dessert usually gets a good response — most parents are happy to contribute. Someone always brings brownies or something. Set a budget for the pizza and split it with the club if they've got any funds, or ask parents for a couple of quid each to cover it.

yeah the club might cover some of it ill ask. this is going to be sick though honestly. some of these kids have never had anyone celebrate them like this. im getting emotional thinking about it and its not even happened yet haha. alright im going to start planning this properly. thanks for helping me sort it out

You're doing something really special for those kids — an evening like that sticks with you. Some of them will remember this years from now. Get the date locked in, send the WhatsApp to parents, order the trophies, and the rest will fall into place. Enjoy it, you've earned it too. Good luck with it all.
